region_of_origin: East Asia; widely consumed in Singapore, Hong Kong, and China

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general abalone food product entry; no specific dried/dehydrated abalone term exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJL covers abalone as a seafood category; no dried-specific sub-code available. Closest defensible match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for dried abalone as a food substance. Left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of dried abalone is approximately 25 g (one small piece), which after soaking and rehydration yields roughly 50–60 g of cooked abalone.

nutrient_source.title: Dried Abalone — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F03040026 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95. All per_100g values are taken from this source without modification.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson, Foster-Powell & Brand-Miller
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for dried abalone in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI estimated at ~40 (low) by analogy with other high-protein, moderate-carbohydrate dried seafood and shellfish products. The high protein content (38 g/100 g) substantially attenuates postprandial glucose response. GL per serving calculated as: (40 × 23.8 × 0.25) / 100 = 2.4 g per 25 g serving. This is an analogous estimate and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content at 2900 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Dried abalone is contraindicated for individuals with hypertension due to its sodium content of 2900 mg/100 g, which far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g threshold; even after prolonged soaking and cooking, residual sodium remains very high, and consumption should be avoided or strictly limited to occasional portions of 5–10 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (23.8 g/100 g) mitigated by very high protein (38 g/100 g) and low GI (~40)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dried abalone has a moderate carbohydrate content of 23.8 g/100 g but its very high protein content (38 g/100 g) significantly slows gastric emptying and moderates postprandial glucose response; typical small portions (20–25 g dried) result in low absolute carbohydrate intake, making it acceptable for most people with Type 2 Diabetes when not prepared with sweet sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high sodium (2900 mg/100 g) and high cholesterol (390 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dried abalone poses a high cardiovascular risk due to its extremely elevated sodium content (2900 mg/100 g), which promotes hypertension and arterial stiffness, combined with a high cholesterol content of 390 mg/100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or very strictly limit consumption and prefer fresh or lightly processed abalone alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium at 2900 mg/100 g exceeds RA threshold; cholesterol at 390 mg/100 g raises inflammatory cardiovascular risk
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dried abalone is unsuitable for regular consumption by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its very high sodium content (2900 mg/100 g), which worsens RA-related tissue swelling and inflammation, and its high cholesterol (390 mg/100 g), which elevates cardiovascular inflammatory risk in RA patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried abalone is a premium processed seafood product made from abalone (Haliotis spp.), a large marine gastropod mollusc, that has been cleaned, salted, and dehydrated to extend shelf life and concentrate flavour. The drying and curing process dramatically reduces moisture content and concentrates all nutrients, particularly protein (38 g/100 g), sodium (2900 mg/100 g), and cholesterol (390 mg/100 g). Before consumption, dried abalone must be soaked in water for 24–72 hours and then braised or steamed, often in rich savoury sauces such as oyster sauce or abalone sauce. The texture of rehydrated dried abalone is firm, chewy, and slightly gelatinous, with a deep umami flavour prized in Chinese and Singaporean cuisine. Dried abalone is considered a luxury ingredient and is commonly served at Chinese New Year banquets, wedding dinners, and other festive occasions in Singapore, Hong Kong, and mainland China. Nutritionally, it is an excellent source of protein and provides meaningful amounts of potassium (490 mg/100 g), calcium (39 mg/100 g), and iron (2 mg/100 g). However, its extremely high sodium content (2900 mg/100 g) makes it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The high cholesterol content (390 mg/100 g) is also a concern for cardiovascular patients. Despite its moderate carbohydrate content (23.8 g/100 g), the very high protein content and low estimated glycemic index (~40) mean that blood glucose impact per typical serving is modest. Dietary fibre is negligible (0 g/100 g). Sugar content is effectively zero. Dried abalone is halal and gluten-free but contains mollusc allergens.
common_user_queries: Is dried abalone high in sodium?
How much protein is in dried abalone?
Is dried abalone suitable for hypertension?
What is the glycemic index of dried abalone?
How do you prepare dried abalone?
What are the health benefits of dried abalone?
How does dried abalone compare to fresh abalone nutritionally?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F03040026, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). All core macros and key micronutrients are present. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence reduced for GI component only) as no direct measurement exists for dried abalone.
notes: 1. sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — dried abalone contains no added or intrinsic sugar detectable at this level. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — abalone is a mollusc with no plant-based fibre. These are confirmed zero values, not unsupported zeros. 2. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, and moisture data for dried abalone are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this record, and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for an exact dried abalone match as of 2026. 3. Input record description field contained an erroneous description referencing 'pork and beef sausage seasoned with paprika and chilli peppers' — this is clearly a data entry error in the source record. The food_name, food_group, food_subgroup, and per_100g values are consistent with dried abalone and have been retained. The description has been corrected in this output. 4. GI value of ~40 is an analogous estimate based on high-protein dried seafood analogy; no direct peer-reviewed GI measurement for dried abalone was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per 25 g serving = (40 × 23.8 × 0.25) / 100 = 2.4 g. 5. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03302765 and FoodEx2 A0CJL are approximate matches; no exact dried abalone ontology code exists in either system as of 2026.
